pykasso package¶

Submodules¶

pykasso.fracture module¶

class pykasso.fracture.Fracture(ID, family, position, radius, orientation, dip, normal)¶

Bases: object

Class modeling fractures as objects.

get_ID()¶

Return the ID of the fracture.

Examples

>>> i = frac.get_ID()
get_dip()¶

Return the dip of the fracture.

Examples

>>> dip = frac.get_dip()
get_family()¶

Return the family ID of the fracture.

Examples

>>> family = frac.get_family()
get_normal()¶

Return an array with the (a, b, c) vector component of the normal of the fracture.

Examples

>>> a, b, c = frac.get_normal()
get_orientation()¶

Return the orientation of the fracture.

Examples

>>> orien = frac.get_orientation()
get_position()¶

Return an array with the (x, y, z) coordinates of the center of the fracture.

Examples

>>> x, y, z = frac.get_position()
get_radius()¶

Return the radius of the fracture.

Examples

>>> rad = frac.get_radius()

pykasso.functions module¶

Common functions can be wrote and grouped here.

pykasso.functions.get_settings(example=None)¶

Provide the datafiles settings.

Parameters

example (str, optionnal) – ‘betteraz’.

Examples

>>> pk.get_settings(example='betteraz')
pykasso.functions.loadpoints(text)¶

Load points in a text. Generally combined with ‘opendatafile()’.

pykasso.functions.opendatafile(file_location)¶

Read a file.

pykasso.geologymanager module¶

class pykasso.geologymanager.GeologyManager(grid)¶

Bases: object

Class modeling the geologic data of the studied domain.

compute_stats_on_data(data_key)¶

Compute statistics (occurence, frequency and superficy) on the geologic data.

Parameters

data_key (str) – Type of data : ‘geology’, ‘faults’, ‘fractures’.

Examples

>>> geol.compute_stats_on_data("geology")
>>> print(geol.data["geology"]["stats"])
generate_fractures(fractures_densities, fractures_alpha, fractures_min_orientation, fractures_max_orientation, fractures_min_dip, fractures_max_dip, fractures_min_length, fractures_max_length)¶

Generate fractures as Fracture instances according to the parameters.

Parameters

  • fractures_densities (array) – Fracture densities for each fracture family.

  • fractures_alpha (array) – Degree of power law for each fracture family.

  • fractures_min_orientation (array) – Fractures minimum orientation for each fracture family.

  • fractures_max_orientation (array) – Fractures maximum orientation for each fracture family.

  • fractures_min_dip (array) – Fractures minimum dip for each fracture family.

  • fractures_max_dip (array) – Fractures maximum dip for each fracture family.

  • fractures_min_length (array) – The minimum lenght of the fractures for each fracture family.

  • fractures_max_length (array) – The maximum lenght of the fractures for each fracture family.

Examples

>>> geol.generate_fractures(fractures_densities = [0.00005,0.0001],
                            fractures_alpha = [2, 2],
                            fractures_min_orientation = [340, 70],
                            fractures_max_orientation = [20, 110],
                            fractures_min_dip = [80, 40],
                            fractures_max_dip = [90, 50],
                            fractures_min_length : [ 100, 100],
                            fractures_max_length : [4000, 4000])
>>> print(geol.fractures)
generate_orientations(surface)¶

Generate maps of x and y components of orientation.

Parameters

surface (2D numpy-array) – A 2D array of the elevation or potential in cell, used to calculate the orientation (i.e. slope or dip) of that cell. Use either the land surface (‘topography’), the surface of the bottom of the karst unit (‘contact’), or the potential returned by the geologic model.

Examples

>>>
rasterize_fracture_network()¶

Rasterize a set of fractures on a 3D grid.

Returns

raster_fractures – With value 1 where the grid is touched by a fracture

Return type

3D numpy array

set_data_from_csv(data_key, datafile_location)¶

Set data from a csv file for the indicated data key in the ‘data’ dictionnnary attribute. Delimiter is ‘,’.

If Grid.nz > 1, the layer is horizontally repeated.

x direction : from West to East y direction : from North to South

Parameters

  • data_key (str) – Type of data : ‘geology’, ‘topography’, ‘surface’ (orientation), ‘faults’ or ‘fractures’.

  • datafile_location (str) – Path of the datafile.

Examples

>>> geol.set_data_from_csv('geology', 'geology.csv')
>>> print(geol.data['geology'])
set_data_from_gslib(data_key, datafile_location)¶

Set data from a gslib file for indicated data key in the ‘data’ dictionnnary attribute.

x-direction : from west to east y-direction : from south to north z-direction : from bottom to top

Parameters

  • data_key (str) – Type of data : ‘geology’, ‘faults’ or ‘fractures’.

  • datafile_location (str) – Path to the datafile.

Examples

>>> geol.set_data_from_gslib('geology', 'geology.gslib')
>>> print(geol.data['geology'])
set_data_from_image(data_key, datafile_location)¶

Set data from an image for a indicated data key in the ‘data’ dictionnnary attribute. The size of the image should be the same that the size of the grid but this is optional. If nz > 1, the layer is horizontally repeated. This method usage is not recommended, it should be used only for quick testing.

Parameters

  • data_key (str) – Type of data : ‘geology’, ‘faults’ or ‘fractures’.

  • datafile_location (str) – Path of the datafile.

Examples

>>> geol.set_data_from_image('geology', 'geology.jpg')
>>> print(geol.data['geology'])
set_data_null(data_key)¶

Set data to ‘null’ for the indicated data key in the ‘data’ dictionnnary attribute.

For ‘topography’, ‘orientationx’ and ‘orientationy’ set : np.zeros((nx,ny))

Otherwise set : np.zeros((nx,ny,nz))

Parameters

data_key (str) – Type of data : ‘geology’, ‘faults’, ‘fractures’, ‘topography’, ‘orientationx’ or ‘orientationy’.

Examples

>>> geol.set_data_null('geology')
>>> print(geol.data['geology'])
show(data='geology', cmap='gray_r')¶

Show data of the geology manager.

Parameters

  • data (str || array, optional) – Data to show : ‘geology’, ‘topography’, ‘orientationx’, ‘orientationy’ ‘faults’ or ‘fractures’. By default, all data are showed.

  • cmap (str, optional) – Color map, ‘gray_r’ by default.

Examples

>>> geol.show()

pykasso.grid module¶

class pykasso.grid.Grid(x0, y0, z0, nx, ny, nz, dx, dy, dz)¶

Bases: object

Class modeling the structured grid of the studied domain.

get_i(x)¶

Retrieve i-index with x-coordinate.

Parameters

x (float) – x-coordinate to test.

Returns

result – i-index.

Return type

int

Examples

>>> i = grid.get_i(3.141)
get_j(y)¶

Retrieve j-index with y-coordinate.

Parameters

y (float) – y-coordinate to test.

Returns

result – j-index.

Return type

int

Examples

>>> j = grid.get_j(3.141)
get_k(z)¶

Retrieve k-index with z-coordinate.

Parameters

z (float) – z-coordinate to test.

Returns

result – k-index.

Return type

int

Examples

>>> k = grid.get_k(3.141)
get_x(i)¶

Retrieve x-coordinate with i-index. Return none type if i-index is outside the grid.

Parameters

i (int) – i-index to test.

Returns

result – x-coordinate.

Return type

float

Examples

>>> x = grid.get_x(42)
get_y(j)¶

Retrieve y-coordinate with j-index. Return none type if j-index is outside the grid.

Parameters

j (int) – j-index to test.

Returns

result – y-coordinate.

Return type

float

Examples

>>> y = grid.get_y(42)
get_z(k)¶

Retrieve z-coordinate with k-index. Return none type if k-index is outside the grid.

Parameters

k (int) – k-index to test.

Returns

result – z-coordinate.

Return type

float

Examples

>>> z = grid.get_z(42)
is_inbox(x, y, z)¶

Test if a (x, y, z) point is inside the grid.

Parameters

  • x (float) – x-coordinate.

  • y (float) – y-coordinate.

  • z (float) – z-coordinate.

Returns

result – 1 if True.

Return type

bool

Examples

>>> bool = grid.inbox(0.53, 11.4, 6.0)

pykasso.karstnetwork module¶

class pykasso.karstnetwork.KarstNetwork(maps, points, network, stats, settings)¶

Bases: object

Class modeling the computed karst network and containing all the data used to get it.

pykasso.pointmanager module¶

class pykasso.pointmanager.PointManager(grid, polygon, geology=None)¶

Bases: object

Class modeling the inlets and the outlets of the studied karst network’s domain.

composite_points(points_key, points, points_number, geological_IDs=None)¶

Generate random points on the grid, and add user indicated points.

Parameters

  • points_key (str) – Type of points : ‘inlets’ or ‘outlets’.

  • points (str || array) – Location of the datafile or list of the points coordinates.

  • points_number (int) – Number of points to generate.

  • geological_IDs (array, optionnal) – List of geologic facies where points can be created.

Examples

>>> pts.composite_points("inlets", "inlets.csv", 20)
>>> pts.composite_points("outlets", [[25,25]], 3, geological_IDs=[1])
generate_points(points_key, points_number, geological_IDs=None)¶

Generate random points on the grid according to the parameters.

Parameters

  • points_key (str) – Type of points : ‘inlets’ or ‘outlets’.

  • points_number (int) – Number of points to generate.

  • geological_IDs (array, optionnal) – List of geologic facies where points can be created.

Examples

>>> pts.generate_points("inlets", 20)
>>> pts.generate_points("outlets", 3, geological_IDs=[0,2])
inspect_points()¶

Check if the points are well located inside the grid. If there is no print out, so everything is OK.

Examples

>>> pts.inspect_points()
set_points(points_key, points)¶

Set new points.

Parameters

  • points_key (str) – Type of points : ‘inlets’ or ‘outlets’.

  • points (str || array) – Location of the datafile or array of the points coordinates like [[x1,y1],[x2,y2],[xn,yn]].

Examples

>>> pts.set_points("inlets", [[0,0], [10,10]])
>>> pts.set_points("outlets", "outlets.csv")
show()¶

Show the delimitation of the grid, of the polygon (if present) and of the locations of the points (if present).

Examples

>>> pts.show()

pykasso.polygon module¶

class pykasso.polygon.Polygon(grid)¶

Bases: object

Class modeling the catchment delimitation of the studied domain.

clean_polygon()¶

Remove the polygon. Set the ‘polygon’ and ‘mask’ attributes to none type.

Examples

>>> poly.clean_polygon()
inspect_polygon()¶

Check if the vertices of the polygon are located inside the grid or not. If all the vertices are outside the grid, the polygon is reseted to none.

Returns

result – Array of vertices out of the grid, else none type.

Return type

array || None

Examples

>>> poly = pk.Polygon(grid)
>>> poly.set_polygon("polygon.csv")
>>> out_pts = poly.inspect_polygon()
set_polygon(vertices)¶

Create a polygon from vertices coordinates. The polygon is stored in ‘polygon’ attribute. It also create a mask and store it in ‘mask’ attribute.

Parameters

vertices (str || array) – Location of the datafile or array of the vertices coordinates like [[x1,y1],[x2,y2],[xn,yn]].

Examples

>>> poly = pk.Polygon(grid)
>>> poly.set_polygon([[0,0], [0,10], [10,0]])
>>> poly.set_polygon("polygon.csv")
>>> print(poly.polygon)
>>> print(poly.mask)
show()¶

Show the delimitation of the grid and, if a polygon is present, display its limits.

Examples

>>> poly.show()

pykasso.sks module¶

pyKasso¶

pyKasso is an object-oriented library intented for karst network simulation.

License¶

Released under the MIT license:

Copyright (C) 2020 Univertsity of Neuchâtel - CHYN François Miville <francois.miville@unine.ch> Chloé Fandel <cfandel@email.arizona.edu> Philippe Renard <philippe.renard@unine.ch>

class pykasso.sks.SKS(yaml_settings_file=None, rand_seed=None)¶

Bases: object

Super-class managing all the data classes and simulating stochastic karst networks.

compare_stats(data=None, mean=False)¶

Compare statistics between reference indicators and calculated networks.

compute_karst_network(verbose=False)¶

Compute the karst network according to the parameters.

Save the results in the karst_simulations attribute as an array.

get_algorithm()¶
get_contact_surface()¶

Get the lower contact surface of the karst unit as a numpy-array.

get_cost_aquiclude()¶
get_cost_aquifer()¶
get_cost_conduits()¶
get_cost_faults()¶
get_cost_fractures()¶
get_cost_out()¶
get_cost_ratio()¶
get_data_has_polygon()¶

Get the boolean value which indicates if a polygon is used or not.

Examples

>>> has_polygon = catchment.get_data_has_polygon()
get_dx()¶

Get the width of cells in the x direction used in the model.

Examples

>>> dx = catchment.get_dx()
get_dy()¶

Get the width of cells in the y direction used in the model.

Examples

>>> dy = catchment.get_dy()
get_dz()¶

Get the width of cells in the z direction used in the model.

Examples

>>> dz = catchment.get_dz()
get_faults()¶

Get the faults data as a numpy-array.

get_faults_datafile()¶
get_faults_mode()¶
get_fractures(fractures_family=None)¶

Get the fractures as a numpy-array.

fractures_familyinteger

First family is 0

get_fractures_alpha()¶
get_fractures_datafile()¶
get_fractures_densities()¶
get_fractures_max_dip()¶
get_fractures_max_length()¶
get_fractures_max_orientation()¶
get_fractures_min_dip()¶
get_fractures_min_length()¶
get_fractures_min_orientation()¶
get_fractures_mode()¶
get_fractures_numbers()¶

Get the number of fractures.

Returns

A dictionnary with the number of fractures given by user, and with the number of fractures calculated.

Return type

dictionnary

get_geological_datafile()¶
get_geological_mode()¶
get_geology()¶

Get the geological data as a numpy-array.

get_geology_cost()¶
get_geology_id()¶
get_importance_factor()¶
get_inlets()¶

Get the inlets as an array.

get_inlets_data()¶
get_inlets_importance()¶
get_inlets_mode()¶
get_inlets_number()¶
get_inlets_per_outlet()¶
get_inlets_shuffle()¶
get_mask()¶

Get the numpy-array mask calculated if a polygon is given.

get_nx()¶

Get the number of nodes in the x direction used in the model.

Examples

>>> nx = catchment.get_nx()
get_ny()¶

Get the number of cells in the y direction used in the model.

Examples

>>> ny = catchment.get_ny()
get_nz()¶

Get the number of cells in the z direction used in the model.

Examples

>>> nz = catchment.get_nz()
get_orientation()¶

Get the orientation data as two numpy arrays (for x and y components).

get_orientation_datafile()¶
get_orientation_mode()¶
get_outlets()¶

Get the outlets as an array.

get_outlets_data()¶
get_outlets_importance()¶
get_outlets_mode()¶
get_outlets_number()¶
get_outlets_shuffle()¶
get_polygon_data()¶

Get the vertices of the polygon.

Examples

>>> polygon_vertices = catchment.get_polygon_data()
get_polygon_vertices()¶

Get the polygon vertices as a list.

get_rand_seed()¶
get_topography()¶

Get the topography data as a numpy-array.

get_topography_datafile()¶
get_topography_mode()¶
get_verbosity()¶
get_x0()¶

Get the origin x-coordinate used in the model.

Examples

>>> x0 = catchment.get_x0()
get_y0()¶

Get the origin y-coordinate used in the model.

Examples

>>> y0 = catchment.get_y0()
get_z0()¶

Get the origin z-coordinate used in the model.

Examples

>>> z0 = catchment.get_z0()
increment_rand_seed()¶

Increment by one the value of the random seed.

set_algorithm(algorithm)¶

Define the algorithm to use when calculating travel time to spring.

algorithmstring

‘Isotropic2’ - ‘Isotropic3’ - ‘Riemann2’ - ‘Riemann3’ - See AGD-HFM documentation for full list of options.

set_cost_aquiclude(cost_aquiclude)¶

Define the fast-marching value for the aquiclude cells. Should be between 0 and 1 and higher than aquiclude but lower than cost_out

cost_aquiclude : float (default: 0.8)

set_cost_aquifer(cost_aquifer)¶

Define the fast-marching value for the aquifer cells. Should be between 0 and 1 and lower than aquiclude but higher than conduits.

cost_aquifer : float (default: 0.3)

set_cost_conduits(cost_conduits)¶

Define the fast-marching value for the conduits cells. Should be between 0 and 1 but lower than aquifer (for conduits to preferentially follow each other)

cost_conduits : float (default: 0.01)

set_cost_faults(cost_faults)¶

Define the fast-marching value for the faults cells. Should be between 0 and 1 and between conduits and cost_out. Higher = conduit will avoid faults, lower = conduits will follow faults

cost_faults : float (default: 0.2)

set_cost_fractures(cost_fractures)¶

Define the fast-marching value for the fractures cells. Should be between 0 and 1 and between conduits and cost_out. Higher = conduit will avoid fractures, lower = conduits will follow fractures

cost_fractures : float (default: 0.2)

set_cost_out(cost_out)¶

Define the fast-marching value for the outside of the study area. The value must be between 0 and 1 and should be high to avoid unrealistic conduits.

cost_out : float (default: 0.999)

set_cost_ratio(cost_ratio)¶

Define the fast-marching ratio of travel cost parallel to gradient / travel cost prependicular to gradient. Should be between 0 and 0.5.

cost_ratio : float (default: 0.25)

set_data_has_polygon(data_has_polygon)¶

Define if study area will be focused on a polygon delimitations.

data_has_polygonbool

If true, a polygon will be required.

set_faults_datafile(faults_datafile)¶

Define the faults datafile path. Useful only when the mode for faults is on ‘gslib’, ‘csv’ or ‘image’.

faults_datafilestring

Faults datafile path.

set_faults_mode(faults_mode)¶

Define the mode for the faults.

faults_modestring

‘null’ - No faults ‘gslib’ - Import faults via gslib ‘csv’ - Import faults via csv ‘image’ - Import faults via image

set_fractures_alpha(fractures_alpha)¶

Define alpha, a parameter in the fracturation law. Useful only when the mode for fractures is on ‘random’.

fractures_alphalist

One alpha for each family.

set_fractures_datafile(fracture_datafile)¶

Define the fractures datafile path. Useful only when the mode for fractures is on ‘gslib’, ‘csv’ or ‘image’.

fracture_datafilestring

Fractures datafile path.

set_fractures_densities(fractures_densities)¶

Define the fractures densitiy for each fractures family. Useful only when the mode for fractures is on ‘random’.

fractures_densitieslist

One density for each family.

set_fractures_max_dip(fractures_max_dip)¶

Define the maximum dip of the fracturation for each fractures family. Useful only when the mode for fractures is on ‘random’.

fractures_max_diplist

One dip for each family.

set_fractures_max_length(fractures_max_length)¶

Define the maximum lenght for all the fractures. Useful only when the mode for fractures is on ‘random’.

fractures_max_length : float

set_fractures_max_orientation(fractures_max_orientation)¶

Define the maximum orientation of the fracturation for each fractures family. Useful only when the mode for fractures is on ‘random’.

fractures_max_orientationlist

One orientation for each family.

set_fractures_min_dip(fractures_min_dip)¶

Define the minimum dip of the fracturation for each fractures family. Useful only when the mode for fractures is on ‘random’.

fractures_min_diplist

One dip for each family.

set_fractures_min_length(fractures_min_length)¶

Define the minimum lenght for all the fractures. Useful only when the mode for fractures is on ‘random’.

fractures_min_length : float

set_fractures_min_orientation(fractures_min_orientation)¶

Define the minimum orientation of the fracturation for each fractures family. Useful only when the mode for fractures is on ‘random’.

fractures_min_orientationlist

One orientation for each family.

set_fractures_mode(fractures_mode)¶

Define the mode for the fractures.

fractures_modestring

‘null’ - No fractures ‘gslib’ - Import fractures via gslib ‘csv’ - Import fractures via csv ‘image’ - Import fractures via image ‘random’ - Generate fractures randomly

set_geological_datafile(geological_datafile)¶

Define the geological datafile path. Useful only when geological mode is not ‘null’.

geological_datafilestring

Geological datafile path.

set_geological_mode(geological_mode)¶

Define the geological mode.

geological_modestring

‘null’ - No geology ‘gslib’ - Import geology via gslib ‘csv’ - Import geology via csv ‘image’ - Import geology via image

set_geology_cost(geology_cost)¶

Define the travel cost to apply to each geology id.

geology_cost : list

set_geology_id(geology_id)¶

Define the geology id (from geology datafile) to consider in the simulation. Only needed in ‘gslib’ or ‘csv’ mode.

geology_id : list

set_inlets_data(inlets_data)¶

Define the inlets datafile path. Useful only when inlets mode is on ‘import’ or ‘composite’.

inlets_datastring or list

Inlets datafile path or list of inlets coordinates.

set_inlets_importance(inlets_importance)¶

Define the proportion of inlets to be distributed across each iteration. Length of array indicates number of inlet iterations, each integer indicates number of inlets to run in that iteration, sum of integers = total number of inlets Useful only when iterating over inlets.

inlets_importance[1]: a single iteration with all inlets, [1,1,1]: three iterations with one inlet in each,

[1,2,3]: three iterations with one inlet in the first, 2 inlets in the second, and 3 inlets in the third.

set_inlets_mode(inlets_mode)¶

Define the inlets mode.

inlets_modestring

‘random’ - Full random points ‘import’ - Import points ‘composite’ - Add n random points to imported points

set_inlets_number(inlets_number)¶

Define the number of inlets to generate. Useful only when inlets mode is on ‘random’ or ‘composite’.

inlets_numberstring

Number of inlets to generate.

set_inlets_per_outlet(inlets_per_outlet)¶

Define the proportion of inlets to be distributed across each outlet. Length of array indicates number of outlets, each integer indicates number of inlets to assign to that outlet, sum of integers = total number of inlets Useful only when iterating over inlets and outlets.

inlets_per_outlet[1]: a single iteration with all inlets to one outlet, [1,1,1]: three outlets with one inlet in each,

[1,2,3]: three outlets with one inlet in the first, 2 inlets in the second, and 3 inlets in the third.

set_inlets_shuffle(inlets_shuffle)¶

Define whether to shuffle the order of the inlets randomly. Useful only when iterating over inlets.

inlets_shufflestring

0: don’t shuffle, 1: shuffle randomly.

set_orientation_datafile(orientation_datafile)¶

Define the orientation datafile path. Usefull only when orientation mode is not ‘null’.

orientation_datafilestring

orientation datafile path.

set_orientation_mode(orientation_mode)¶

Define the orientation mode.

orientation_modestring

‘null’ - No orientation ‘topo’ - Calculate from topography ‘surface’ - Calculate from csv file of a surface (useful if using lower surface of karst unit)

set_outlets_data(outlets_data)¶

Define the outlets datafile path. Useful only when outlets mode is on ‘import’ or ‘composite’.

outlets_datastring or list

Outlets datafile path or list of outlets coordinates.

set_outlets_importance(outlets_importance)¶

Define the proportion of outlets to be distributed across each iteration. Length of array indicates number of outlet iterations, each integer indicates number of outlets to run in that iteration, sum of integers = total number of outlets Useful only when iterating over outlets.

outlets_importance[1]: a single iteration with all outlets, [1,1,1]: three iterations with one outlet in each,

[1,2,3]: three iterations with one outlet in the first, 2 outlets in the second, and 3 outlets in the third.

set_outlets_mode(outlets_mode)¶

Define the outlets mode.

outlets_modestring

‘random’ - Full random points ‘import’ - Import points ‘composite’ - Add n random points to imported points

set_outlets_number(outlets_number)¶

Define the number of outlets to generate. Useful only when outlets mode is on ‘random’ or ‘composite’.

outlets_numberstring

Number of outlets to generate.

set_outlets_shuffle(outlets_shuffle)¶

Define whether to shuffle the order of the outlets randomly. Useful only when iterating over outlets.

outlets_shufflestring

0: don’t shuffle, 1: shuffle randomly.

set_polygon_data(polygon_data)¶

Define the polygon datafile path. Useful only when data_has_polygon is true.

polygon_datastring or list

Polygon datafile path or list of vertices coordinates.

set_rand_seed(rand_seed)¶

Define the random seed. May help for reproduicity.

rand_seed : integer

set_topography_datafile(topography_datafile)¶

Define the topography datafile path. Useful only when topography mode is not ‘null’.

topography_datafilestring

topography datafile path.

set_topography_mode(topography_mode)¶

Define the topography mode.

topography_modestring

‘null’ - No topography ‘gslib’ - Import topography via gslib ‘csv’ - Import topography via csv

set_verbosity(verbosity)¶

Define the verbosity (how much output to print during runs).

verbosity: integer

0: print minimal output, 1: print medium output, 2: print max output

show(data=None, title=None, probability=False)¶

Show the entire study domain (defaults to showing most recent simulation).

show_catchment(data='geology', title=None, cmap='binary')¶

Show the entire study domain.

Parameters

  • data (str, optional) – Data to show : ‘geology’, ‘topography’, ‘orientationx’, ‘orientationy’ ‘faults’ or ‘fractures’. By default : ‘geology’.

  • title (str, optional) – Title of the plot. If ‘None’, ‘data’ becomes the label.

  • cmap (str, optional) – Color map, ‘binary’ by default.

show_network(data=None, simplify=False, ax=None, plot_nodes=True, polygon=True, labels=['inlets', 'outlets'], title=None, cmap=None, color='k', alpha=1, legend=True)¶

#Chloe: This is a new function that I use to create all the figures for the paper. Show the karst network as a graph with nodes and edges. Defaults to showing latest iteration.

Parameters

  • data – karst simulation object containing nodes, edges, points, etc. Can be obtained from self.karst_simulations[i]

  • ax – axis to plot on

  • label – None or list of strings [‘nodes’,’edges’,’inlets’,’outlets’], indicating which components to label

  • title (str) – title of plot

  • cmap (str) – colormap to use when plotting

  • color (str) – single color to use when plotting (cannot have both cmap and color)

  • alpha (float) – opacity to plot with (1=opaque, 0=transparent)

  • legend (bool) – whether to display legend

  • plot_nodes (bool) – whether to display nodes

  • polygon (bool) – whether to display the bounding polygon

shuffle_inlets()¶

Shuffle the inlets order.

shuffle_outlets()¶

Shuffle the outlets order.

update_all()¶

Update all the parameters.

update_faults()¶

Update the faults settings.

update_fractures()¶

Update the fractures settings.

update_geology()¶

Update the geology settings.

update_inlets()¶

Update the inlets settings.

update_orientation()¶

Update the orientation settings.

update_outlets()¶

Update the outlets settings.

update_polygon()¶

Update the polygon settings.

update_topography()¶

Update the topography settings.
